sentences.text: Oh, that's right.
Yes.
Well, as things became bad, my parents rented two rooms, where early in my life, my grandfather and grandmother had, had stayed with us.
And we had a number of, of people who rented till they could find an apartment.
And one was a family by the name of [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was an actor at the Schauspielhaus in Munich.
And they became really good friends after they moved.
And in fact, when we had a maid who was not allowed anymore after-- I'm not sure which, which year-- if anybody had a maid that was under 35 years old could not stay in a Jewish household.
And so our maid left.
But when she had been with us, she had always made me a Christmas tree, I'm sure as much for her pleasure as for mine.
Well, these people, [NON-ENGLISH], after the maid left the first Christmas, they came and brought me a tree because they didn't want me to be deprived.
They were not Jewish, no.
And another family was by the name of Gunter.
And she was a lady, a New Zealander.
And her husband was half German and half English.
And they stayed with us for a while till they had found an apartment.
And later on, they moved back to New Zealand and came back to Germany, back and forth, and were life long, long time friends of my parents.
And in 1939, they had to emigrated England.
They were non-Jewish.
But they had to leave Germany suddenly, because they were both musicians, but not practicing.
They lived from their income, which was from England.
Because they had been, I guess, wealthy people in England.
And they lived just from their income.
And when you see the regulations were mostly, mostly against Jews.
But there were also other regulations.
And one was that you could not have money overseas.
You had to bring it into Germany.
And these people didn't want to bring their fortune into Germany.
So they left in the middle of the night and left everything behind and moved to England.
And when they read about the Kristallnacht, they wrote to my parents immediately, that what can they do for us?
And they made their home available to my parents.
And subsequently, well, my mother, you know, it wasn't as easy as they thought, that you could just get out.
But they offered my parents a home in their home.
And my parents never made it out.
But they did take me in.
And I lived with them.
And they treated me as a daughter.
Yes.
We are.
Well, just to say that these are the people that were originally.
And then there was one other family that I know of.
And they were-- he was a Chinese doctor.
And his wife was German.
And they also offered to help us after the war.
So that was a short time of different people living in our home.
Well, altogether, after Kristallnacht.
But before that, I was not quite the only one in the school, but the only one in my class, as I said, because of, of all these regulations.
Also some were already emigrating, and so on.
So there were less and less Jewish children.
Also, there were all kinds of places where you couldn't go.
Like you couldn't go swimming.
We couldn't go to the, to the skating rink.
All these things that were forbidden to Jews.
Well, that is a good question, in the view of it.
I mean, you were known in the area.
But also, it is a good question because I must say, only my mother of all the people I know did this.
She thought it was very healthy to go swimming.
And she would send me in any case, and nobody interfered with me.
And I would go to this, to this, you know, pool.
But always, I was a lot by myself because, of course, you know, nobody interfered with me.
But on the whole, if it says, no Jews allowed, you really didn't go, which applied to more and more things.
Yeah, there were signs.
And also more and more friends who I had who were not Jewish.
Like, there was a little boy when we were little living downstairs.
And we were friends.
Well, of course, then boys and girls went to separate schools anyway.
So there was less, you know, less time to be together.
But eventually, you know, all these people would keep away from you, even if they were not anti-Semitic per se.
It was just sort of understood.
And so you became more and more isolated.
Yes.
I mean, you, you knew that, you know, that there were these regulations and these laws against the Jews.
And they became more stringent.
And I think people here don't understand that it kept up.
It didn't happen all at once.
And my parents and their friends, they used to talk, oh, well, you know, that-- that Hitler is a flash in the pan, and we'll outlive this regime.
And, you know, and didn't really realize that it was going to be progressively getting worse.
Yeah, well, up to that time, as I said, things became that you were more uneasy and more regulations against Jews.
They had to take all the silver and turn it in, you know.
And they had to--
Yes.
And they had to-- well, businesses.
And I told you the maids could not stay.
And businesses were failing.
And people-- doctors couldn't practice anymore, or rather not on non-Jewish patients.
Well, that is-- it was doing worse.
But they still had, you know, had customers.
It's-- lawyers were prohibited, you see.
People that were working for the state as-- as [NON-ENGLISH]-- what's [NON-ENGLISH]?
You know, the function of the government-- government employees lost their jobs.
So a private business, as long as the customers were still buying, was still sustainable.
At that time, my mother started.
She was never working.
She would go in Saturday mornings with my fathers, I guess maybe to work on the books or whatever.
So eventually, you know, just economic conditions became worse for Jews.
What else did you ask me?
Well, I was asking you what you remembered about Kristallnacht and what happened that night?
Yes.
Oh, oh, that.
Yes.
OK, to lead up to that, is a-- I was, at that time, to school.
We had six years of high school.
And all of a sudden, Germany, I guess, already preparing for war, cut the school years, I guess, to get people to go in the army or whatever.
And so after the fifth year, we graduated.
So my parents at that time wanted me to go some more to school.
And I went to this one year to that for women's studies, where I explained to you where I learned to cook and such things.
And one of the other things that they did in the school was that for six weeks, I were sent to learn nursing-- not nursing care-- like a children's nursery, you know, child care and stuff like that.
So when the other non-Jewish children went to, to a hospital where they learned about taking care of babies, I was sent to a Jewish home which was orphanage.
It was partly for an orphanage, or also a home for maybe working mothers, you know, and so on.
So I was sent there.
And while I was there was when Kristallnacht occurred.
And the night-- that's why I mentioned before about living across from Hitler-- possibly because of living there, nothing occurred in our neighborhood.

So the synagogue-- I should still say, the synagogue in Munich was torn down some years earlier because they decided they going need a garage, a parking-- what do you-- not a parking garage, but the parking lot for the German-- the House of German Kunst, you know, art exhibit or whatever.
And they tore down the synagogue.
So in my neighborhood, which was residential, nothing happened.
So the next morning, I went to school to that orphanage where I was supposed to take care of babies and children.
And sometime in the morning, this lady, who I said had at one time lived with my parents, this Chinese-- the wife of this Chinese doctor, came to pick me up from school.
Because this was the morning after Kristallnacht.
And I guess when it became-- oh, we had no radio in those days.
So by-- by-- I guess by the time they found out something was going on, they had to bring me home from school.
And I was fetched to come home from school.
This other woman, yes, this friend had came to pick me up.
So I came home.
And, you know, little by little, by word of mouth, we found out that the stores in the, in the city had been torn, you know, ransacked and what had happened in town.
And they were arresting the German men.
So whether it was-- I guess it was later that day or the next, they came for my father, the stormtroopers.
And my father wasn't home.
That's what I don't-- I'm sorry.
I don't know if he was in his business or not.
I really don't know where he was, but he wasn't home.
And this is in retrospect, I'm using, is that I had been taught, I guess, never to let anybody into the house without asking for identification.
And I had the nerve to ask these two stormtroopers to show me identification.
And they did.
And they were, you know, when you stand up-- stood up to them, they were a little-- I mean, I don't say intimidated from a 16-year-old girl, but taken aback maybe.
And so they asked for, for my father.
I said, my mother said, my father isn't home.
And we didn't know where he was or whatever.
And they said, well, he better show himself at the main-- I don't know if that's a police station or whatever.
He had to report there.
And then they left quite politely.
And you know, they left.
But of course, when my father came home, like a good German that follows orders, he presented himself at, at whatever-- whether it was a gestapo headquarters or wherever.
And of course, he was interned.
And he was in the Dachau concentration camp for four weeks.
They took-- when he went there, he took-- I mean, you know, my parents, I guess, they were law abiding.
They didn't think of trying to go across.
Well, besides that, I mean, we would have been targets.
Anyway, my father went.
And he was four weeks in concentration camp.
And it was the coldest winter, very early winter.
Already, it was cold.
I remember every morning looking what, what temperature.
And of course, he didn't have warm enough clothes.
And after four weeks, he came back, and of course with a shaven head.
And they were all instructed not to say anything, which they didn't, you know, about the conditions.
I have to go back.
When my father was in the concentration camp, in one of our apartments lived-- well, there was the stormtroopers and there were the SR.
I'm trying to remember to say what this is in English.
The brown shirted ones, you see?
And one of these dignitaries lived in our, in one of our apartments.
And my mother asked him if he could help to get my father out.
And he said he would make inquiries.
And he lived a floor above us.
And we heard him come home.
And you know, you had this little thing where you could look out through the-- see who in an apartment.
And we heard him come home with his storm troop boots.
And the man had the courtesy to change into civilian clothes.
And he came to our house.
And he told my mother that he was-- had been in Dachau.
And he will try to get-- do something.
But of course, nothing ever happened.
Whether he didn't try or he couldn't, I don't know.
But four weeks later, my father came back.
Well, as I said to you, they were instructed not to tell anything.
And my father was only telling funny stories, except they weren't so funny.
They only-- though they don't tell-- didn't tell anything.
The only story was that my father had very, very nice teeth.
And when they were running to-- oh, he said, he knew never to be the last, because the last one gets beaten.
But he always made it sound so funny.
And he said, so he was always the first.
And in the washroom, one of the stormtroopers called him out.
And he said, those teeth, are they your own?
And my father said, well, he was thinking quickly.
If he tells him they're his own, he'll knock them out.
And if he tells him they're not his own, he'll also knock them out.
So he said, no, they're his own.
And they guy said, they're nice teeth.
And he let him go.
And the other story my father told, that they were calling, when you were 60, over 60, you were released.
And so one morning, they called roll call.
And they called David Pappenheimer.
My father had a cousin who was also the same name.
And my father said he knew very well that the other one was 60 and my father wasn't.
But he stepped forward anyway, thinking he could get out.
But the real David Pappenheimer stepped out, and they let him go.
And my father had to stay till after four weeks.
Now, my father's sister lived in Switzerland.
And the day after he was interned, she managed to get a visa from Switzerland for him.
And we went to the Swiss consulate.
My mother and I.
And there were hundreds of people waiting.
And we were allowed in right away.
And we had the visa.
But my father was in, in camp.
So they said, when you bring out your pop and you bring us your passport, we'll enter the visa and you can leave for Switzerland.
And then when my father came out, the next thing was that they had taken the passports.
They has-- in Munich-- not in every city.
In Munich, they confiscated all the passports.
And my parents didn't get the passport till the war broke out.
And that's why they were never allowed to get-- could never get out.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, earlier, going back much earlier, a cousin of my mother's had sent her daughters to school in-- one in England and one in France.
And she urged my mother to send me out.
And I really didn't like to hear that at all.
And [BACKGROUND NOISE] my mother said, no.
I was too young to send me away.
And I was very glad of that.
And then after, after the Kristallnacht when the kindertransports came into being, another cousin of my mother's, I guess, made her aware if the kindertransport.
And then these people, Gunter in-- in England that had said they would take me in, were approached.
And I would-- I traveled to England with a kindertransport.
Well, naturally by that time, I guess, he couldn't-- I don't know if he had sold his business.
I-- it's-- I think he had to sell his business.
He couldn't keep his business anymore.
And he sold it a non-Jew probably for under the price that it was worth.
I remember, I remember visiting once these people that bought the business.
That my parents went there to visit them, and they lived somewhere in the country.
And we spent some times there.
That I-- I do remember.
I don't really know what he did anymore then.
As I said earlier, he did on Saturdays then go to the temple and so on.
He didn't, you know, have so much to do.
I can't remember if he still went at all to the business.
But naturally, it became evident that we should emigrate.
No, no, no.
That was the last day--
That was the last day.
--that I had gone to school was the day when they, you know, picked me up.
Yeah.
That was the end of school.
Yes.
Well, the people were still-- oh, yes.
People were, you know, all the-- the tenants that were in the house were, were still living there.
And then one day-- now, I'm trying to go back to-- that was in 19-- well, it must have been beginning of 1939 that they came one day.
They came and wanted to take over the apartment.
There were some party dignitaries or whatever.
And they came-- they came and they brought a moving van with them, the Nazis.
And they said, you-- to my father, we need this apartment.
You see, now what came in about it.
We need the party for the party.
You know, we need the apartment for somebody from the party.
And we'll move you.
And as I said, the moving van was outside.
And my father said, no, I'm not leaving this apartment.
And-- and he-- he said to these young Nazis, he said, I fought for this country when you were still doing-- making in your diapers.
And you're not a evicting me from my apartment.
So they said, well, you have to take it up with the party headquarters.
And so my father-- and they moved themselves with the van and left, but saying they'll be back.
So my father went, sure enough, to the party.
And my mother was very nervous.
I remember my mother.
We had, as I said, the apartment had this long hallway where the other the two rooms and the bathroom and the maid's room were.
And there was a curtain, like separating the front of the apartment.
And my mother was behind that curtain, very nervous about how my father was speaking to them.
And then my father left.
And he went to the party headquarters and came to a so-called agreement that, yes, they need this, this apartment for the party.
And they will move us somewhere else.
And in exchange-- oh, and in exchange for, for coming to this compromise, they told my father that he could sell the house on the open market.
Because at that time, you could already-- it was just taken over.
You couldn't-- so it became known in Munich that we could sell the house on the open market.
So my father went with them to look where they were going to be relocated.
And there was an apartment where there was an old Jewish lady living in the, in the city.
And my father said to these two Nazis, well, there's somebody living there.
We can't go there.
There's somebody living there.
And they said, oh, well, her we'll hang.
So the outcome was that they didn't-- that she moved into the back room or another room.
And my parents had to move in there, into these quarters.
And I was shortly-- before I left for England anyway-- I was living with a friend, with some friends meanwhile.
And my parents lived then in this terrible apartment.
And they had-- they packed their belongings because they were expecting to go to Switzerland and eventually also to England.
Because they had this visa for England.
And they sent the whole, what we call the lift, which was a moving crate with the furniture, to England to furnish the room that was supposed to be their room in [NON-ENGLISH] house.
And so with that, that was the resolving of living where we lived.
And my father did sell the house, which later after the war came back to me.
